10: The first Babylonian siege and deportation (597 BCE) — Nebuchadnezzar coming against Jerusalem, Jehoiachin surrendering after three months, the king and his court taken to Babylon — is independently documented in the Babylonian Chronicles (British Museum tablet BM 21946, first published 1956 by Donald Wiseman). The Babylonian Chronicle for Nebuchadnezzar’s seventh year reads: ‘In the seventh year, in the month Kislev, the king of Akkad mustered his troops... and encamped against the city of Judah... on the second day of the month of Adar he seized the city and captured the king. He appointed there a king of his own choice, received its heavy tribute, and sent (them) to Babylon.’ The detail of ‘a king of his own choice’ (Zedekiah) and the tribute match 2 Kings 24:12-17 precisely. The date ‘second day of Adar’ (March 16, 597 BCE) is one of the most precisely dated events in the entire OT, confirmed by an external source. The historical solidity of the 597 siege is the archaeological anchor for understanding the entire exile narrative: Ezekiel’s dates (Ezekiel 1:1-2), Jeremiah’s letters to the exiles (Jer 29), and Daniel’s narrative (Daniel 1:1-3) all presuppose the 597 deportation as their historical foundation.
1: In Jehoiakim’s days Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon comes; Jehoiakim serves him three years before rebelling. Nebuchadnezzar’s decisive victory over Egypt at Carchemish (605 BCE) established Babylonian hegemony over the Levant. The Babylonian Chronicle records Nebuchadnezzar’s first western campaign immediately after Carchemish. Jehoiakim’s initial submission (ca. 605 BCE) matches the standard vassal-treaty pattern; his rebellion (ca. 602 BCE) came after Nebuchadnezzar’s failure against Egypt in 601 BCE.
2: YHWH sends raiding bands of Chaldeans, Arameans, Moabites, and Ammonites against Jehoiakim. The multi-ethnic raiding coalition reflects the imperial strategy of deploying client peoples to punish rebellious vassals before sending the main army. The Babylonian Chronicle confirms Chaldean raids in the western provinces between Nebuchadnezzar’s withdrawal and his 598/597 BCE siege. The theology — YHWH sends them — frames the imperial pressure as covenantal judgment.
3: The editorial aside attributes the raids to the command of YHWH because of the sins of Manasseh and the innocent blood he shed. The theological explanation — inserted into the narrative of Jehoiakim’s rebellion — connects the Babylonian pressure to the deep covenantal debt from Manasseh’s reign. The ‘innocent blood’ (dām nāqî) refers particularly to Manasseh’s judicial murders (2 Kings 21:16) and possibly to the execution of prophets.
4: YHWH is not willing to forgive the innocent blood — he filled Jerusalem with innocent blood. The construction ʾāḇāh lišlōaḥ (literally ‘was not pleased to send away’) emphasizes divine resolution rather than inability. The accumulation of innocent blood as the specific guilt requiring judgment echoes Deuteronomy’s purgation laws (Deuteronomy 19:13; 2 Kings 21:9) which make the land itself guilty if unpunished blood remains. The exile becomes the mechanism by which the land is purged.
5: The regnal closing formula for Jehoiakim directs readers to the Book of the Annals of the Kings of Judah. The compressed treatment of Jehoiakim’s reign in Kings contrasts with the extensive prophetic material in Jeremiah. Kings focuses on the theological meaning of events; Jeremiah provides the social and religious detail. The combination of the two books gives a fuller picture of the period than either alone.
6: Jehoiakim dies and Jehoiachin his son reigns in his place. The note of Jehoiakim’s death is unusually terse — no burial formula, no ‘slept with his fathers.’ This is likely deliberate: Jeremiah’s oracle predicted an ‘ass’s burial’ for Jehoiakim, left unburied outside Jerusalem’s gates (Jeremiah 22:18–19; 2 Kings 36:30). The Babylonian Chronicle mentions the siege of Jerusalem in Nebuchadnezzar’s seventh year (598/7 BCE); Jehoiakim may have died during the siege or just before it.
7: The king of Egypt does not march from his land again because the king of Babylon has taken all that belonged to Egypt from the Brook of Egypt to the Euphrates. The Babylonian Chronicle confirms that after the Battle of Carchemish (605 BCE) and the subsequent campaign of 601 BCE (where Nebuchadnezzar suffered heavy losses but halted Egyptian advance), Egypt’s Levantine possessions were definitively transferred to Babylonian control. The Euphrates-to-Egypt boundary is the traditional delineation of the Levantine sphere.
8: Jehoiachin is eighteen years old at accession, reigning only three months in Jerusalem (598/597 BCE). The parallel with Jehoahaz’s three-month reign underscores the dynastic collapse: two successive Davidic kings reigned only three months each before being deposed by foreign powers. The regnal formula dates his accession as the convergence of Nebuchadnezzar’s siege and Jehoiakim’s death.
9: Jehoiachin does evil in YHWH’s sight like everything his father had done. The brief and negative evaluation of Jehoiachin is mitigated by his later rehabilitation in the book’s final episode (2 Kings 25:27–30). The Babylonian administrative tablets (published by E.F. Weidner in 1939) from Nebuchadnezzar’s palace in Babylon list ration allocations for ‘Ia’ukînu, king of the land of Yahudu’ — confirming Jehoiachin’s Babylonian captivity and his continued royal status there.
11: Nebuchadnezzar himself comes to the city while his servants are besieging it. The personal presence of the Babylonian king at the siege — confirmed in the Babylonian Chronicle: ‘in the seventh year, in the month Kislimu, the king of Akkad mustered his army and marched to Hattu-land’ — marks the campaign’s importance. The Chronicle records that Nebuchadnezzar ‘seized the city of Judah [and] on the second day of the month Addaru he appointed a new king of his own choice’.
12: Jehoiachin goes out to Nebuchadnezzar along with his mother, servants, princes, and court officials. The voluntary surrender of Jehoiachin — ‘going out’ rather than being captured — spared Jerusalem the total destruction that would come a decade later under Zedekiah. The surrender preserved the city’s walls, the majority of the population, and left the temple standing. This is the standard capitulation pattern for a Babylonian siege.
13: Nebuchadnezzar carries off all the treasures of the temple and palace, cutting up all the gold vessels Solomon made for the temple. The cutting (qiṣṣaṣ) of the gold vessels makes them untraceable and easier to transport as bullion. This is the first taking of the temple vessels — a partial plunder; the more systematic stripping occurs in the 586 BCE destruction (2 Kings 25:13–17). The prophetic word in Isaiah 39:6 (to Hezekiah) had predicted this Babylonian appropriation.
14: Nebuchadnezzar exiles all Jerusalem — all officials, warriors (10,000 total), and craftsmen and metalworkers — leaving only the poorest people. The selective deportation strategy targets the leadership and skilled workforce, leaving only those incapable of effective resistance. This Babylonian practice — confirmed in Assyrian administrative records — achieves political pacification while retaining the agricultural workforce needed for tax revenue.
15: Nebuchadnezzar carries Jehoiachin to Babylon, along with the king’s mother (haggᵉḇîrāh), the king’s wives, his court officials, and the chief men of the land. The prominent mention of the gᵉḇîrāh (queen mother) reflects the institutional importance of the office in the Davidic court. The Babylonian ration tablets confirm that Jehoiachin’s sons also received rations, suggesting the royal family was kept together.
16: The deportees include 7,000 men of valor, 1,000 craftsmen and metalworkers — all strong and fit for war. The concentration on the military and artisan classes reflects the same deportation strategy documented in Assyrian records: removing the political, military, and technical capacity that might organize resistance. Jeremiah 29:1–2 lists the exiled group as elders, priests, prophets, the king and queen mother, court officials, and craftsmen.
17: The king of Babylon makes Mattaniah, Jehoiachin’s uncle, king in his place and renames him Zedekiah. The renaming — from Mattaniah (‘gift of YHWH’) to Zedekiah (‘YHWH is my righteousness’) — is again the suzerain’s act of naming his vassal, claiming sovereignty over his identity. Zedekiah is Josiah’s third son to reign (after Jehoahaz and Jehoiakim); he is Jehoiachin’s uncle, not his father.
18: Zedekiah is twenty-one years old at accession, reigning eleven years in Jerusalem (597–586 BCE). His mother Hamutal from Libnah is the same as Jehoahaz’s mother (2 Kings 23:31), making Zedekiah and Jehoahaz full brothers and Jehoiakim their half-brother. Zedekiah’s eleven years — parallel to Jehoiakim’s eleven years — end in the catastrophe of the siege and destruction that Jehoiakim’s rebellion triggered.
19: Zedekiah does evil in YHWH’s sight according to all that Jehoiakim had done. The compressed evaluation of Zedekiah in Kings contrasts with the extensive narrative in Jeremiah showing Zedekiah’s wavering — personally interested in Jeremiah’s counsel but ultimately unable to resist his officials’ pressure (Jeremiah 38:14–26). Kings focuses on the verdict; Jeremiah documents the tragic indecision.
20: It was because of YHWH’s anger that the things which happened to Jerusalem and Judah happened, until he cast them from his presence — and Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon. The editorial pivot — divine anger explaining events, followed immediately by the rebellion that triggers the final destruction — binds theology and history. Zedekiah’s rebellion (ca. 589 BCE) likely involved Egyptian encouragement: Pharaoh Hophra (Apries) had promised military support (Jeremiah 37:5).
